advaita.short_description: Non-dualism: Brahman alone is ultimately real; the world's plurality is vyāvahārika and ultimately mithyā (neither sat nor asat).

vishishtadvaita.short_description: Qualified non-dualism: one Brahman (Viṣṇu) qualified by real cit and acit modes, with the śarīra-śarīri-bhāva relating souls and world to the Lord.

dvaita.short_description: Tattvavāda: Viṣṇu alone is svatantra (independent); all else is paratantra (dependent), with five eternal real distinctions (pañca-bheda).

bhedabheda.short_description: Difference-and-non-difference: Brahman is intrinsically both identical with and distinct from souls and world; classical (aupādhika) and natural (svābhāvika) variants.

acintya.short_description: Inconceivable difference-and-non-difference: the Gauḍīya thesis that Bhagavān's simultaneous identity-and-difference with souls and world is irreducibly acintya.

shuddha.short_description: Pure non-dualism: Brahman and world are non-different without invoking māyā; world is an avikṛta-pariṇāma of Brahman's essential nature.

avibhaga.short_description: Non-difference-without-identity: Vijñānabhikṣu's harmonizing thesis that Brahman and world are inseparable yet not numerically one.

proto.short_description: Pre-Śaṅkara figures known mostly through citation by later authors, evidencing a plural Vedāntic field before the extant bhāṣyas.

trika-comparator.short_description: Kashmir Śaiva recognition-school: included as a structural comparator (svātantrya, divine self-veiling) and not as a Vedāntic school.

kashmir-shaiva-dualist.short_description: The dualist Kashmirian Saiddhāntika tradition (Sadyojyotis, Bhaṭṭa Rāmakaṇṭha): pati, paśu, and pāśa as three eternally distinct realities, defended against the non-dual Pratyabhijñā position; distinct from the southern Tamil Śaiva-Siddhānta.

shakta-srividya.short_description: The Śrīvidyā Śākta tradition centred on Tripurā-Sundarī, the śrī-cakra, and the Lalitā-Sahasranāma; in its early-modern form synthesised with Advaita Vedānta by Bhāskararāya.

shakta-kalakula.short_description: The Krama / Kālīkula Śākta tradition (Mahārtha lineage): worship of Kālī as the sequenced (krama) self-display of consciousness, systematised in the South by Maheśvarānanda.

cross-tradition.short_description: Non-Vedāntic figures (e.g., Bhartṛhari) included only insofar as their thought feeds Vedāntic argument.

samkhya-comparator.short_description: The dualist enumerationist school: twenty-five tattvas, satkārya-vāda, puruṣa-prakṛti dualism; the Vedānta-Sūtra's first major opponent at BS 2.1.1 ff.

nyaya-comparator.short_description: The classical realist epistemology: sixteen padārthas, four pramāṇas, five-membered inference; the standard Vedāntic dialogue partner on perception and inference.

navya-nyaya-comparator.short_description: Gaṅgeśa's reformed Nyāya: technical idiom of vyāpti, avacchedaka, paryāpti that becomes the dialectical lingua franca of late Vedāntic polemic from the fourteenth century onward.

mimamsa-comparator.short_description: The ritual-exegetical realist tradition (Jaimini, Śabara, Bhāṭṭa, Prābhākara) on śabda as autonomous pramāṇa and self-validating cognition; sister-darśana whose categories Vedānta both inherits and contests.

yoga-comparator.short_description: Pātañjala-Yoga: the praxis-darśana that holds the Sāṃkhya twenty-five-tattva ontology within an eight-limbed disciplinary frame, admits īśvara as a special puruṣa, and supplies the standard pre-modern phenomenology of samādhi inherited by every Vedāntic school.

vaisesika-comparator.short_description: The categorial-realist atomist darśana: the six (later seven) padārthas — dravya, guṇa, karma, sāmānya, viśeṣa, samavāya (and abhāva) — and a paramāṇu-causal cosmology refuted at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.11–17 yet redeployed positively by Madhva.

jaina-comparator.short_description: The Jaina tradition: anekānta-vāda, syād-vāda, and the two-naya structure (vyavahāra/niścaya) whose formal parallel with the Advaita vyāvahārika/pāramārthika distinction is structurally instructive and is engaged at Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.33–36.

carvaka-comparator.short_description: The reconstructed position holding pratyakṣa as sole pramāṇa, denying inference's reliability and the post-mortem soul; preserved only through hostile citation in Mādhava's Sarva-Darśana-Saṅgraha and in Jayarāśi's Tattvopaplava-Siṃha.

madhyamaka-comparator.short_description: Nāgārjuna's middle-way Buddhism: śūnyatā-as-pratītya-samutpāda, the catuṣkoṭi as load-bearing dialectical apparatus, and the two-truths (saṃvṛti / paramārtha) whose structural homology with Advaita's vyāvahārika / pāramārthika drives the pracchanna-bauddha charge against Śaṅkara.

yogacara-comparator.short_description: The Buddhist cognition-only school of Asaṅga and Vasubandhu: trisvabhāva (parikalpita / paratantra / pariniṣpanna), ālaya-vijñāna as transformative substrate, and vijñapti-mātratā — refuted at Brahma-Sūtra-Bhāṣya 2.2.28–32 and engaged extensively by Yāmuna's Saṃvit-Siddhi.

buddhist-pramana-comparator.short_description: The Dignāga–Dharmakīrti epistemological school: the pramāṇa-dvaya thesis (only pratyakṣa and anumāna), apoha-vāda for concept-formation, the svalakṣaṇa / sāmānya-lakṣaṇa distinction, and trairūpya for the inferential mark; the principal Buddhist dialogue partner of Naiyāyikas Uddyotakara, Vāchaspati, Udayana, and Gaṅgeśa.

sarvastivada-comparator.short_description: The Abhidharmic schools whose foundational thesis sarvam asti (past, present, and future dharmas exist as such, in their respective temporal modes) is the principal target of the Vedāntic refutation at Brahma-Sūtra 2.2.18–20; the Sautrāntika critique (Vasubandhu's auto-commentary) and Saṅghabhadra's orthodox Vaibhāṣika defence (Nyāyānusāra) constitute the internal Buddhist debate the Vedāntic critique inherits.

theravada-comparator.short_description: The Pāli Theravāda tradition systematised by Buddhaghosa (Visuddhimagga, Aṭṭhasālinī, Sumaṅgala-Vilāsinī): anattā as the eliminative refutation of substance-self, the paramattha-sacca / sammuti-sacca distinction, and the twelve-link paṭicca-samuppāda chain; minimally engaged by Vedāntic authors directly but indispensable as the southern-Buddhist comparator for understanding what is and is not at stake in Mādhyamaka and Yogācāra readings of dependent origination.

tathagatagarbha-comparator.short_description: The Buddha-nature literature (Ratna-Gotra-Vibhāga / Uttara-Tantra-Śāstra and the Maitreya-attributed corpus; the Laṅkāvatāra-Sūtra) articulating tathāgatagarbha as universally present, prakṛti-prabhāsvara-citta (naturally luminous mind), and the eka-yāna doctrine; not a substantialist self but a non-substantialist account of the cognitive ground of awakening, decisive for understanding Pratyabhijñā / Trika's inheritance into Kashmir Śaivism and Vedānta-Deśika's Śatadūṣaṇī engagement.
